Wisconsin has created prison-release avenues that place trial judges in the position of back-end decision-makers. Sentencing courts have unusual powers to vary the degrees of indeterminacy in their sentences. They also have sentence “modification” and “adjustment” powers that can be exercised long into the life of a prison sentence. Prison sentences are bifurcated into an initial “term of confinement” and a separate term of post-release supervision called the “extended supervision term.” In most cases, the timespan of the confinement term may never exceed 75 percent of the judicial maximum term (“total bifurcated sentence”) and the extended supervision term may not be less than 25 percent of the total.







In 2020, there were people in prison past their parole eligibility year. This group made up of people in prison for new crimes and with sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.

From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 11%, changing from 22,724 in 2010 to 20,202 in 2022.








Source: BJS Prisoners Series

























Source: BJS Prisoners 2022 - Race and Ethnicity




































From 2010 to 2020, prison releases decreased 3%, dropping from 9,444 in 2010 to 9,155 in 2020.





















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions.















The demographics of people released from prison reveal notable proportions among White, non-Hispanic and black, non-hispanic people. Gender distribution indicates a predominance of males over females. Age-wise, the majority of people were 25-34 years and 35-44 years old. These findings provide insights into the populations transitioning back into the community.

























Between 2010 and 2020, there were shifts in the average time served by individuals for different offense types in Wisconsin. The largest change was for murder and non-negligent manslaughter offenses, which increased by 89%.














In Wisconsin, Black, non-Hispanic people are incarcerated in state prison at a rate 14 times higher and Hispanic, any race people are incarcerated in state prison at a rate 2.7 times higher and Other race(s), non-Hispanic people are incarcerated in state prison at a rate 2.7 times higher than White, non-Hispanic people, when accounting for population sizes in the community.

Hispanic, any race individuals faced the longest average time served in prison in 2020, with an average of 3.5 years. Other race(s), non-Hispanic individuals experienced shorter prison stays, averaging 1.8 years.








By offense type, disparities were observed in time served by race and ethnicity. For Murder and Non-negligent Manslaughter offenses, Hispanic, any race individuals had 11.4 more years on average compared to Other race(s), non-Hispanic individuals, who had the shortest time served for these offenses.

Vermont judges have discretion to vary the relationship between the minimum and maximum prison terms they impose. In most cases both the judicial minimum and maximum are subject to potential reductions through credit earnings. Starting in 2021 earned time credits of 7 days per month reduce both minimum and maximum. Serious “listed” offenses are subject to traditional discretionary parole while unlisted offenses are subject to presumptive more automatic release.

Uses two major categories by offense. The lower-severity group become eligible for parole when their time served plus good time credits equals one-quarter of the maximum or 15 years whichever is less. The higher severity group become eligible for parole at one-half half of the maximum or 30 years whichever is less and without reduction by good time. Good time credits are in the 10 to 20 days per month range based on classification. Good time can advance mandatory release but the parole board has discretion to veto mandatory release.







In 2020, there were 35,668 people in prison past their parole consideration year. This group made up 52% of people in prison for new crimes and with sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.













The demographics of people in prison past their parole consideration year are mostly notable proportions among Hispanic, any race and White, non-Hispanic people. By gender, there were more males than females. Age-wise, most people were 25-34 years and 35-44 years old.
























In 2020, 56% of people in prison past their parole consideration year were in prison for violent offenses. The breakdown of criminal offenses reveals a more varied landscape, with most people incarcerated for aggravated or simple assault (20%) and robbery (16%) offenses.














In 2020, most people in prison past their parole consideration year had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years representing 49% of those eligible for parole.

By statute, there is a continuum of staggered parole-release formulas from 25-percent to 75-percent mark based on offense severity and criminal history. (For crime committed after July 1, 2023, sentences for designated serious crimes have been made non-parolable.) The state also uses an “administrative parole release” system that streamlines the procedural path to release compared with traditional parole frameworks. A large percentage of people in prison in South Dakota are assigned “initial parole dates” shortly after admission with the expectation that they will be released without a hearing before the parole board if they comply with the requirements of their “individual program directives.” Discharge credits could total as much as 270 days per year advancing mandatory release 43 percent.







In 2020, there were 34 people in prison past their parole consideration year. This group made up 2% of people in prison for new crimes and with sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.

In 2020, 76% of people in prison past their parole consideration year were in prison for non-violent offenses. The breakdown of criminal offenses reveals a more varied landscape, with most people incarcerated for property (38%) and drugs (29%) offenses.














In 2020, most people in prison past their parole consideration year had original sentence lengths between 2 to 4.9 years representing 50% of those eligible for parole.

Most prison sentences in New Hampshire include judicial minimum and maximum terms. Judicial minimum term may not exceed one-half of the maximum term. people in prison can earn up to 12.5 days per month for good conduct.







In 2020, there were 238 people in prison past their parole consideration year. This group made up 37% of people in prison for new crimes and with sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.













The demographics of people in prison past their parole consideration year are mostly notable proportions among White, non-Hispanic and Hispanic, any race people. By gender, there were more males than females. Age-wise, most people were 35-44 years and 25-34 years old.
























In 2020, 51% of people in prison past their parole consideration year were in prison for non-violent offenses. The breakdown of criminal offenses reveals a more varied landscape, with most people incarcerated for rape or sexual assault (23%) and property (22%) offenses.














In 2020, most people in prison past their parole consideration year had original sentence lengths between 2 to 4.9 years representing 37% of those eligible for parole.

For maximum terms no longer than four years, prison sentences have no parole release eligibility. Maximum terms of 20 years or more almost always include parole release eligibility with reductions for good time credits; this group of people in prison becomes eligible for discretionary parole release after serving one-half of their judicial minimum terms. This means that no matter how long a minimum term the judge may have selected and because the minimum may not exceed the maximum first parole eligibility will always come at the 50-percent mark of the judicial maximum sentence or earlier.







In 2020, there were 29 people in prison past their parole consideration year. This group made up 1% of people in prison for new crimes and with sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.













The demographics of people in prison past their parole consideration year are mostly notable proportions among Black, non-Hispanic and White, non-Hispanic people. Gender distribution data is incomplete or missing. Age-wise, most people were 35-44 years and 25-34 years old.
























In 2020, 52% of people in prison past their parole consideration year were in prison for non-violent offenses. The breakdown of criminal offenses reveals a more varied landscape, with most people incarcerated for public order (24%) and robbery (17%) offenses.














In 2020, most people in prison past their parole consideration year had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years representing 93% of those eligible for parole.

Minimum terms to parole release eligibility for general-rules people in prison are not fixed by statute in Missouri but are established by regulations promulgated by the parole board with a range from 15 percent to 33 percent of the maximum. By statute dangerous offenses serve 85 percent and repeated commitments to prison for other serious offenses result in 40, 50, and 70 percent minimums.







In 2020, there were 2,796 people in prison past their parole consideration year. This group made up 22% of people in prison for new crimes and with sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.













The demographics of people in prison past their parole consideration year are mostly notable proportions among White, non-Hispanic and Black, non-Hispanic people. By gender, there were more males than females. Age-wise, most people were 25-34 years and 35-44 years old.
























In 2020, 57% of people in prison past their parole consideration year were in prison for violent offenses. The breakdown of criminal offenses reveals a more varied landscape, with most people incarcerated for rape or sexual assault (29%) and property (19%) offenses.














In 2020, most people in prison past their parole consideration year had original sentence lengths between 5 to 9.9 years representing 44% of those eligible for parole.

In 2020, there were 1,339 people in prison past their parole consideration year. This group made up 28% of people in prison for new crimes and with sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.













The demographics of people in prison past their parole consideration year are mostly notable proportions among Hispanic, any race and White, non-Hispanic people. By gender, there were more males than females. Age-wise, most people were 25-34 years and 35-44 years old.
























In 2020, 52% of people in prison past their parole consideration year were in prison for non-violent offenses. The breakdown of criminal offenses reveals a more varied landscape, with most people incarcerated for drugs (29%) and robbery (14%) offenses.














In 2020, most people in prison past their parole consideration year had original sentence lengths between 2 to 4.9 years representing 45% of those eligible for parole.

title: The Discretionary Power of Parole
text:
State leaders across the United States are grappling with multiple public safety challenges, including shortages in the correctional workforce, limited access to behavioral health services, and the ongoing task of supporting successful reentry for individuals returning from incarceration. Parole release discretion plays a pivotal role in addressing these challenges within 34 state justice systems.
In 2020, there were 88,000 people in prison past their parole eligibility year, highlighting the critical role that parole boards play in determining the timing of release. The accumulation of people who are eligible but not released underscores the importance of timely and appropriate parole decisions, which are crucial for managing prison capacity and ensuring that correctional systems can operate effectively amid staffing and resource constraints.

title: The Discretionary Power of Parole
text:
The Power of Parole Boards
Despite the critical role they play, parole boards are often composed of a small number of individuals. In many states, just 5-10 parole board members are responsible for decisions affecting thousands of people awaiting parole.Parole board members must balance a range of factors, including public safety, the rehabilitation progress of incarcerated people, and the perspectives of victims and their families. These boards often work with limited resources and under significant public and political pressure.

title: Arnold Ventures Parole Project
text:
The Council of State Governments (CSG) Justice Center intends to illuminate parole release policies and practices in the United States and how they impact prison population sizes and lengths of stay, using data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) from up to 45 states to:

Examine trends in their parole-eligible populations;
Conduct original empirical research on racial, ethnic, and gender disparities on the portion of sentences that are served in carceral settings past parole eligibility; and
Conduct deep dives into selected states to shed light on parole board decision-making. For these states, we will seek to identify any disparate decision-making and associated outcomes and recommend changes to policy and practice to prevent unnecessarily long prison stays while maintaining public safety.

For general-rules people in prison, first eligibility for discretionary parole release occurs at the 25-percent mark of incarcerated people’s judicial maximum sentences. Montana is one of a small number of states that have abolished good time, earned time, and related credits for people in prison to reduce their judicial maximum sentences or the period of time to first parole-release eligibility.

title: Montana
text: For general-rules people in prison, first eligibility for discretionary parole release occurs at the 25-percent mark of incarcerated people’s judicial maximum sentences. Sentencing courts in Montana have the unusual power to order in individual cases that people in prison will never enjoy eligibility for discretionary release or that their minimum terms to release eligibility will be delayed past the 25-percent mark of their maximum sentences. Montana is one of a small number of states that have abolished good time, earned time, and related credits for people in prison to reduce their judicial maximum sentences or the period of time to first parole-release eligibility.

Most people in prison are serving “determinate” sentences but a substantial number are serving “indeterminate” sentences. Determinate sentences are for higher levels of seriousness and they receive a judicial maximum that can only be reduced with credits topping out at 14 percent discount. Indeterminate sentences receive a judicial minimum and maximum with no statutory constraint on the ratio between them. people in prison serving indeterminate sentences are eligible for parole “at any time” after the expiration of their minimum sentences which may be advanced by as much as 17 percent for merit time credits.







In 2020, there were 1,178 people in prison past their parole consideration year. This group made up 6% of people in prison for new crimes and with sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.

The Oklahoma parole board has authority to grant discretionary parole release only to people in prison convicted of nonviolent offenses. For people in prison convicted of “violent offenses” the parole board is limited only to making recommendations to release to the governor. The release decision itself must be made by the governor; it is the only state where the governor is the ultimate decision-maker in ordinary parole-release cases for large numbers of people in prison. For offenses committed between July 1, 1998 and November 1, 2018 people in prison become eligible after serving one-third of their judicial maximum sentences. For offenses committed on or after November 1, 2018 people in prison become eligible after one-fourth of their judicial maximum terms. people in prison convicted of nonviolent offenses are eligible for more streamlined “administrative parole.” Violent offenses are subject to 85 percent eligibility. Earned credits can reach 60 days per month for nonviolent and 44 days per month for violent.

Black people faced the longest average time served in prison in 2020, with an average of 2.7 years. Other races people experienced shorter prison stays, averaging 1.8 years.








By offense type, disparities were observed in time served by race and ethnicity. For robbery offenses, Black people had 8 more years on average compared to people of Other* races, who had the shortest time served for these offenses.

title: Georgia
text:
In 2020, there were 11,827 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 46 percent of people* in prison.








From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 12 percent.















Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2020, most people in prison past their parole eligibility were Black, non-Hispanic people, representing 56 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people in prison past their parole eligibility were males, representing 93 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility.










In 2020, most people in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old, representing 39 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility.


















In 2020, 59% of people in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses. The breakdown of criminal offenses reveals a more varied landscape, with most people incarcerated for aggravated or simple assault (26%) and property (19%) offenses.















In 2020, most people in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 66% of in prison past parole eligibility.
